flashcards.back: A vision is long-term and about the journey; an idea is short-term and objective-based. Tesla illustrates this by selling the vision of an exciting future, not merely an electric car.

flashcards.front: Name five online tools that can be used to create a pitch presentation.
flashcards.back: PowerPoint, Google Slides, Keynote, Canva, and Prezi.

flashcards.front: What is traction in a pitch presentation and why does it matter to investors?
flashcards.back: Traction is evidence of real progress — proof points and milestones already achieved. It matters because it reduces investor fear by demonstrating that the business idea works in practice.

blanks.clue: Design thinking is an iterative, ____ approach used to understand users, challenge assumptions, and create innovative solutions.
In the ____ phase, designers research and understand the users' needs through user research.
In the Define phase, the team states the users' needs and pins down the ____ problem.
During the ____ phase, designers challenge assumptions and generate many possible solutions.
The Prototype phase involves building experimental, ____ versions of the proposed solutions.
In the Test phase, results are used to refine either the ____ or the solution.
Design thinking is particularly valuable for tackling ____ problems through innovation.
The overall aim of design thinking is to turn ideas into testable ____ quickly and try them on real customers.

flashcards.front: List all 5 phases of Design Thinking in order.
flashcards.back: 1. Empathize – research users' needs. 2. Define – state needs and pin down the core problem. 3. Ideate – challenge assumptions and generate solutions. 4. Prototype – build low-cost experimental versions. 5. Test – try prototypes with users and use results to refine the problem or solution.

flashcards.front: What is the definition of Design Thinking and its core aim?
flashcards.back: Design Thinking is an iterative, human-centred approach used to understand users, challenge assumptions, and create innovative solutions. Its aim is to turn ideas into testable prototypes quickly and test them on real customers.

flashcards.front: What specifically happens in the Ideate phase of Design Thinking?
flashcards.back: Designers challenge existing assumptions and generate as many possible solutions to the defined problem as they can.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the Prototype phase in Design Thinking?
flashcards.back: To build experimental, low-cost versions of the proposed solutions so they can be tested with real users before committing to full development.

flashcards.front: What are the three business benefits of applying Design Thinking?
flashcards.back: 1. It builds deep empathy for customers. 2. It leads to solutions that match real needs. 3. It is effective for tackling unclear or ambiguous problems through innovation, improving products, services, and internal processes.

flashcards.front: What is the key output of the Define phase and how does it differ from Empathize?
flashcards.back: Empathize gathers raw understanding of users' needs through research; Define synthesises that research into a clear, actionable problem statement that pinpoints the core problem to solve.

flashcards.front: Why is Design Thinking described as iterative?
flashcards.back: Because the Test phase feeds results back into the process — findings are used to refine either the problem definition or the solution, meaning teams can cycle through the phases multiple times rather than following a strictly linear path.

blanks.clue: Business corporations consist of commercial companies and ____.
A limited-liability company (s.r.o.) comes into legal existence upon ____ in the commercial register.
In an s.r.o., each monetary contribution must be paid up at least ____% before registration.
The minimum registered capital for a joint-stock company (a.s.) is CZK ____.
In an unlimited partnership (v.o.s.), all partners are ____ liable with all their property for company debts.
A cooperative must have at least ____ members.
In a cooperative with fewer than 50 members, the ____ acts as the statutory body instead of a board of directors.
A trade is defined as a systematic activity carried out independently, in one's own name, on one's own responsibility, and for ____.
Licensed trades (koncesovaná živnost) require a granted ____ and proven professional competence before the business may operate.
After dissolution, a company's assets and debts are wound up through ____, unless the company enters insolvency proceedings.

flashcards.front: List all types of commercial companies under Czech law and classify them as partnerships or capital companies.
flashcards.back: Partnerships: unlimited partnership (v.o.s.) and limited partnership (k.s.). Capital companies: limited-liability company (s.r.o.) and joint-stock company (a.s.).

flashcards.front: What are the two governance structures available for a Czech joint-stock company (a.s.) and what bodies does each include?
flashcards.back: Dualistic: board of directors + supervisory board. Monistic: statutory director + administrative board.

flashcards.front: Describe the liability rules distinguishing general partners from limited partners in a limited partnership (k.s.).
flashcards.back: General partners are liable with all their personal property (unlimited liability) and form the statutory body. Limited partners are liable only up to the amount of their unpaid contribution.

flashcards.front: List the three mandatory bodies of a cooperative (družstvo) and the function of each.
flashcards.back: Members' meeting: supreme body. Board of directors: statutory body, manages operations. Audit committee: supervisory body, controls the board.

flashcards.front: List the three types of notifiable trades (ohlašovací živnosti) and state what distinguishes licensed trades from them.
flashcards.back: Notifiable: craft (řemeslná), qualified (vázaná), and free/unqualified (volná). They begin once the legal conditions are met. Licensed trades (koncesovaná) additionally require a state-granted concession and proven professional competence before operation.

flashcards.front: What are the four defining characteristics of a 'trade' under the Czech Trade Licensing Act?
flashcards.back: It must be: (1) systematic, (2) carried out independently, (3) in one's own name and on one's own responsibility, (4) for profit.

flashcards.front: What is the member liability rule in an s.r.o. and what are its three corporate bodies?
flashcards.back: Members are liable for company debts only up to the amount of their unpaid contribution (limited liability). Bodies: general meeting (supreme), executive(s)/jednatel (statutory), and an optional supervisory board.

flashcards.front: State the contribution rule specific to cooperatives regarding equality and maximum amount.
flashcards.back: Every member's contribution obligation is the same, and it cannot exceed three times the basic membership contribution.

blanks.clue: In the Czech regulatory hierarchy, if a Decree conflicts with the Accounting Act, the ____ wins.
In ____ accounting, one transaction is recorded in two accounts simultaneously.
The ____ principle requires that expenses be matched to the revenues they help generate.
An accounting entity is classified into a size category if it meets at least ____ of the three criteria for that size.
Banks, insurers, and listed companies are classified as ____ entities regardless of their size criteria.
The balance sheet equation states that Assets equal Liabilities plus ____.
The profit and loss statement can be structured either by ____ or by purpose.
Czech Accounting Standards occupy the ____ level of the Czech accounting regulatory hierarchy.
The ____ concept requires that the financial records of the business be kept separate from those of its owners.

flashcards.front: List the three levels of the Czech accounting regulatory hierarchy in descending order of authority.
flashcards.back: 1. Accounting Act (general law); 2. Decrees (specify the Act); 3. Czech Accounting Standards (practical implementation steps). The higher level always prevails in case of conflict.

flashcards.front: What are the three criteria used to classify accounting entities by size in the Czech Republic?
flashcards.back: 1. Net (total) assets; 2. Net annual turnover; 3. Average number of employees. An entity is assigned to a size category if it meets at least two of the three limits for that category.

flashcards.front: Name the four size categories of Czech accounting entities and state the general principle about duties as size increases.
flashcards.back: The four categories are: micro, small, medium, and large. The larger the entity, the more duties it has — including mandatory audit, fuller financial statements, and more extensive disclosure to the commercial register.

flashcards.front: List all five components of the annual financial statements under Czech accounting.
flashcards.back: 1. Balance sheet; 2. Profit and loss statement; 3. Cash-flow statement; 4. Statement of changes in equity; 5. Notes (příloha).

flashcards.front: Describe the four basic transaction types by their effect on the balance sheet.
flashcards.back: 1. Assets ↑ and Equity/Liabilities ↑ (e.g. taking a loan); 2. Assets ↑ and Assets ↓ (e.g. buying inventory for cash); 3. Liabilities/Equity ↑ and Liabilities/Equity ↓ (e.g. converting debt to equity); 4. Assets ↓ and Liabilities/Equity ↓ (e.g. repaying a loan). The balance sheet remains balanced after each type.

flashcards.front: What are the two methods by which a cash-flow statement can be prepared, and what distinguishes double-entry from single-entry accounting?
flashcards.back: Cash-flow statement methods: direct (lists actual cash inflows and outflows) and indirect (starts from net profit and adjusts for non-cash items). Double-entry (podvojné): every transaction is recorded in two accounts. Single-entry (jednoduché): one transaction equals one entry.

flashcards.front: List the six basic accounting principles mentioned in Czech accounting theory.
flashcards.back: 1. Accrual principle; 2. Accounting-entity concept; 3. Periodicity; 4. Matching principle (match expenses to related revenues); 5. One accounting system per entity; 6. Adequate disclosure.

blanks.clue: A tax is a compulsory, legally-set, non-returnable, and ____ payment, meaning you do not receive a direct service in return for the exact amount paid.
The ____ function of taxation describes how progressive tax rates help cool an overheating economy.
The part of the tax construction that defines *who pays* the tax is called the tax ____.
VAT registration becomes mandatory in the Czech Republic once a business's turnover exceeds CZK ____ over the relevant period.
The income tax return deadline is generally extended from 3 months to ____ months if filed by a tax advisor or if the entity is audited.
Alcohol, tobacco, and mineral oils are subject to ____ taxes, which are a type of selective indirect tax.
The ____ function of taxation means that taxes are used to fund the public budget.
Road tax and real-estate tax are examples of ____ taxes within the Czech direct tax system.

flashcards.front: List all five functions of taxes and briefly describe each.
flashcards.back: 1. Fiscal – fund the public budget; 2. Allocating – direct resources in the economy; 3. Redistributive – transfer wealth between groups; 4. Stimulating – encourage behaviour (e.g. tax holidays); 5. Stabilising – progressive taxation dampens economic overheating.

flashcards.front: List all the structural (construction) elements of a tax.
flashcards.back: Tax subject (who pays), tax object (what is taxed), exemptions, tax base, tax period, deductions, tax rate, and tax reliefs.

flashcards.front: How is the Czech tax system classified? Name all main categories and subcategories.
flashcards.back: Direct taxes: income taxes (personal and corporate) and property taxes (real-estate tax, road tax). Indirect taxes: universal (VAT) and selective/excise (alcohol, tobacco, mineral oils; energy taxes), plus customs duty.

flashcards.front: What are the standard income tax return deadlines under the Czech Procedural Tax Code, and under what conditions does the deadline extend?
flashcards.back: Generally 3 months after the end of the tax period; extended to 6 months if the return is filed by a tax advisor or if the entity is subject to a statutory audit.

flashcards.front: What is the standard VAT rate in the Czech Republic, and at what turnover threshold does VAT registration become mandatory?
flashcards.back: The standard VAT rate is 21%. Registration becomes mandatory once turnover exceeds CZK 2,000,000 over the relevant period.

flashcards.front: Define a tax, including all four defining characteristics.
flashcards.back: A tax is a compulsory, legally-set, non-returnable, and non-equivalent payment to a public budget — the payer does not receive a direct, proportional service in return.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between the tax object and the tax base as structural elements of a tax?
flashcards.back: The tax object is what is taxed (e.g. income, property, a transaction), while the tax base is the quantified measure of the object to which the tax rate is applied (e.g. the amount of income in CZK).

blanks.clue: For tax periods starting in 2024, the Czech corporate income tax rate is ____.
A Czech corporate taxpayer seated in the Czech Republic is taxed on ____ income, while a foreign entity is taxed only on Czech-source income.
The corporate income tax base is derived from accounting profit by adding non-deductible costs and subtracting ____ items.
The personal income tax rate of 15% applies to income up to approximately ____ times the average wage annually.
Income above the threshold for personal income tax is taxed at the higher rate of ____.
An individual becomes a Czech tax resident if they have a permanent home in the Czech Republic or spend ____ days or more there in a calendar year.
An employee's total social and health insurance contribution rate is ____, combining 4.5% health and 7.1% social insurance.
The employer's combined contribution for health and social insurance, paid on top of gross salary, is ____.
If a corporate tax return is filed by a tax advisor or if accounts are audited, the filing deadline is extended to ____ months after year-end.

flashcards.front: List the five partial tax bases (§ numbers and categories) that make up the personal income tax base in the Czech Republic.
flashcards.back: §6 employment income, §7 self-employment income, §8 capital gains, §9 rental income, §10 other income.

flashcards.front: What are the two personal income tax rates in the Czech Republic and what threshold separates them?
flashcards.back: 15% applies to income up to approximately 36× the average annual wage; 23% applies to income above that threshold.

flashcards.front: Describe the four-step computation of Czech personal income tax due.
flashcards.back: 1) Determine the total tax base from the five partial bases. 2) Deduct non-taxable parts and allowable deductions. 3) Apply the applicable rate(s) — 15% and/or 23%. 4) Subtract tax reliefs/credits to arrive at the tax due.

flashcards.front: What distinguishes a Czech tax resident from a non-resident in terms of tax liability and the conditions for becoming a resident?
flashcards.back: A resident has unlimited tax liability (taxed on worldwide income); a non-resident has limited liability (taxed only on Czech-source income). Residency is established by having a permanent home in the Czech Republic OR spending 183 or more days there in a calendar year.

flashcards.front: State the current Czech corporate income tax rate and the standard filing deadline for the tax return.
flashcards.back: The rate is 21% (for tax periods starting in 2024). The standard filing deadline is 3 months after year-end; it extends to 6 months if filed by a tax advisor or if the accounts are subject to audit.

flashcards.front: Break down the current Czech employee and employer social and health insurance contribution rates.
flashcards.back: Employee: health 4.5% + social 7.1% = 11.6% total. Employer: health 9% + social 24.8% = 33.8% total (paid on top of gross salary).

flashcards.front: How is the Czech corporate income tax base calculated, and name two categories of income that are not taxed?
flashcards.back: The tax base starts from accounting profit, then non-deductible costs are added back and allowable items are subtracted. Examples of non-taxed income include certain membership fees and income from state funds.

blanks.clue: A business development assessment is a systematic, procedural analysis that reports KPIs and feedback to show the current reality, opportunities, and ____ within an organization.
The ____ approach to change is rationally controlled, follows a timetable, and assumes a smooth path from the current to the desired state.
The ____ approach to change sees change as an ongoing process of adaptation in response to fast-changing conditions such as technology, globalisation, or crises.
In the business development process, the ____ step involves diagnosing the current situation before any interventions are designed.
In the business development process, the ____ step involves gathering data, analysing results, and feeding information back into the cycle.
At the organization level, the assessment process begins with project ____, which includes creating a work plan and holding a kick-off meeting.
During big-picture data collection at the organization level, the primary method used is ____ management in order to gather broad strategic information.
After targeted data collection, the organization-level assessment moves to analysis and identification of strategic ____ before action planning begins.

flashcards.front: State the goal and purpose of a business development assessment.
flashcards.back: It is a systematic, procedural analysis that reports KPIs and feedback to reveal the current reality, opportunities, and gaps, thereby supporting strategic decisions about the future direction of the business.

flashcards.front: List the four steps of the planned business development process in order and state what each step involves.
flashcards.back: 1. Analyze — diagnose the current situation. 2. Plan — design the interventions and actions. 3. Execute — put the plan into action. 4. Evaluate — gather data, analyse results, and feed back.

flashcards.front: Contrast the planned and emergent approaches to change: what is each approach's core assumption?
flashcards.back: The planned approach assumes change is rationally controlled with timetables and a smooth path from current to desired state. The emergent approach assumes change is an ongoing, unplanned process of adaptation driven by fast-changing conditions (technology, globalisation, crises like COVID).

flashcards.front: List, in order, the five stages of a business development assessment at the organization level.
flashcards.back: 1. Project mobilisation (work plan, kick-off). 2. Big-picture data collection (interview management). 3. Targeted data collection (key issues). 4. Analysis and identification of strategic changes. 5. Action planning.

flashcards.front: What distinguishes targeted data collection from big-picture data collection in the organization-level assessment?
flashcards.back: Big-picture data collection gathers broad information by interviewing management, while targeted data collection focuses on specific key issues identified from the initial broad review.

flashcards.front: Give three examples of fast-changing conditions that make an emergent approach to change necessary.
flashcards.back: Technology change, globalisation, and major crises such as COVID-19.

flashcards.front: Why is the evaluate step critical in the planned business development process?
flashcards.back: It closes the loop by gathering data, analysing results, and feeding information back, allowing the organization to assess whether the executed plan achieved the desired state and to inform future cycles.

blanks.clue: According to the open systems model, an organization takes inputs, transforms them, and returns ____ to the external environment.
The concept of ____ describes signs that a firm's internal infrastructure has not kept up with its growth in size.
Flamholtz measures organizational size primarily by ____ when assessing whether growing pains are present.
The gap between a firm's actual organizational development and what its size requires is called the ____ gap.
A key criticism of life-cycle models is that they underweight the ____ factor, because even well-planned organizations make mistakes through their people.
In the organizational life cycle, the phase that follows growth and involves stabilization is called ____.
The open systems model holds that an organization does not operate in isolation but always within an external ____.
One growing pain identified by Flamholtz is that sales grow but ____ do not, signaling infrastructure is lagging.

flashcards.front: List the 4–5 phases of the organizational life cycle in order.
flashcards.back: Startup → Emerging (survival) → Growth → Maturity (stabilisation) → Transformation/Termination.

flashcards.front: Explain the open systems model of an organization in terms of inputs, transformation, and feedback.
flashcards.back: An organization operates within an external environment: it takes inputs from that environment, transforms them internally, and returns outputs back to the environment. The environment's reaction then feeds back into how the organization functions.

flashcards.front: What is the main conceptual criticism of organizational life-cycle models regarding predictability and people?
flashcards.back: Life-cycle models assume organizations develop in a similar and plannable way, but they underweight the human factor — even well-planned organizations make mistakes through their people, making development less predictable than the models suggest.

flashcards.front: Define 'growing pains' as used by Flamholtz, including what they measure and what they indicate.
flashcards.back: Growing pains (Flamholtz) are signs that a firm's internal infrastructure has not kept up with its size (measured by revenue), creating an 'organizational development gap' between where the organization is and where it needs to be.

flashcards.front: List at least five specific growing pains identified by Flamholtz.
flashcards.back: 1. Sales grow but profits do not. 2. Plans are made but not followed up. 3. Employees feel there are 'not enough hours in the day.' 4. People do not know what others are doing or where the firm is headed. 5. Too few good managers — leaders feel they must do things themselves to get them done right. 6. Meetings feel like a waste of time and some employees feel insecure.

flashcards.front: What does the 'organizational development gap' refer to in Flamholtz's framework?
flashcards.back: It is the gap between a firm's actual level of internal organizational development (infrastructure, management systems, processes) and the level required to support its current revenue/size — the core concept behind growing pains.

flashcards.front: In the open systems model, what role does the external environment play beyond simply providing inputs?
flashcards.back: The external environment also receives the organization's outputs and provides feedback that influences the organization's future functioning — meaning the organization is continuously shaped by and dependent on its environment, not self-contained.

blanks.clue: The first step in collecting assessment information is to ____ the problem by choosing the right areas to analyse.
Internal documents, annual reports, and financial statements are examples of ____ data, which is cheap and fast to obtain.
Interviews, focus groups, questionnaires, and mystery shopping are all methods of collecting ____ data.
Quantitative research is associated with ____, meaning it moves from general theory to specific observations.
Qualitative research is associated with ____, meaning it builds general understanding from detailed observations.
Surveys, standardised observation, and simulation are typical methods of ____ research, which relies on a valid sample.
Unstructured questioning, qualitative content analysis, and non-standardised observation are methods of ____ research, which relies on depth of interpretation.
____ shopping is a primary data collection technique in which a researcher poses as a customer to observe service quality.
Analytical frameworks such as SLEPT, Porter's five forces, 7S, and 4P are chosen during the second step of the process: choosing the ____ method.

flashcards.front: List the three steps in the process of collecting and analysing assessment information.
flashcards.back: 1. Define the problem (choose the right areas to analyse). 2. Choose the analytical method (e.g. SLEPT, Porter's five forces, 7S, 4P, brand analysis). 3. Collect and analyse the data (using secondary and/or primary sources).

flashcards.front: Name at least five analytical frameworks/models that can be chosen in the assessment process.
flashcards.back: SLEPT, Porter's five forces, 7S (McKinsey), 4P (marketing mix), and brand analysis.

flashcards.front: What distinguishes secondary data from primary data in terms of sources and practical advantages?
flashcards.back: Secondary data comes from already-existing sources such as internal documents, annual reports, statistics, and financial statements; it is cheap and fast to obtain. Primary data is collected firsthand through interviews, focus groups, questionnaires, observation, or mystery shopping.

flashcards.front: What are the four main methods of quantitative research and what does quantitative research rely on?
flashcards.back: The four main methods are surveys, secondary-data analysis, standardised observation, and testing/simulation. Quantitative research relies on a valid (representative) sample and follows a deductive approach.

flashcards.front: What are the four main methods of qualitative research and what does qualitative research rely on?
flashcards.back: The four main methods are unstructured questioning, qualitative content analysis, non-standardised observation, and experiment. Qualitative research relies on depth of interpretation and follows an inductive approach.

flashcards.front: For what specific purposes are questionnaires versus interviews most commonly used as primary data sources?
flashcards.back: Questionnaires are most commonly used to collect data on demand, preferences, and satisfaction. Interviews are used to explore perceptions, attitudes, and opinions.

flashcards.front: Explain the difference between deduction and induction as they apply to quantitative and qualitative research.
flashcards.back: Deduction (used in quantitative research) starts with a general theory or hypothesis and tests it against specific data. Induction (used in qualitative research) starts with specific observations and builds toward broader general understanding or theory.

blanks.clue: OD interventions are activities and events designed to improve an organization's ____ and effectiveness.
An effective intervention must transfer ____ competence to the organization's members, not just solve the immediate problem.
Implementation means moving from the current state to a desired ____ state.
An ____ plan is the tool that turns strategy into specific projects by defining responsible persons, due dates, and estimated costs.
For each activity in an action plan, three elements must be defined: the responsible person, the due date, and the estimated ____.
When measuring OD effectiveness, financial results are compared across time periods, against competitors, and against ____ averages.
____ is a key financial ratio that measures the return generated relative to the equity invested by shareholders.
Measuring effectiveness requires both qualitative and ____ criteria chosen to fit the organization's size, industry, and philosophy.

flashcards.front: What are the three defining requirements for an OD intervention to be considered effective?
flashcards.back: 1) It must fit the organization's needs. 2) It must be based on solid knowledge of the intended outcomes. 3) It must transfer change-management competence to the organization's members.

flashcards.front: What are the three mandatory elements defined for each activity in an action plan?
flashcards.back: 1) The responsible person. 2) The due date. 3) The estimated cost.

flashcards.front: List the key financial ratios used as indicators of OD effectiveness.
flashcards.back: ROI (return on investment), ROE (return on equity), profit margin, sales growth, and market share.

flashcards.front: Against what three reference points are financial effectiveness measures compared in OD evaluation?
flashcards.back: 1) Across different time periods (trend analysis). 2) Against direct competitors. 3) Against industry averages.

flashcards.front: What is the distinction between standardised and custom OD interventions?
flashcards.back: Standardised interventions are ready-made programs applicable across organizations, while custom interventions are designed specifically for a particular organization's unique needs and context.

flashcards.front: What key performance areas serve as qualitative and quantitative levers when measuring OD effectiveness?
flashcards.back: Key performance levers and value creation, competitive position, financial results, and future cash flow potential.

flashcards.front: What does implementation in OD specifically require beyond executing activities?
flashcards.back: Implementation requires a special management structure, action planning, and actively managing the learning that occurs during the change process.
